observations.recommendation:
We recommended and management agreed that the Municipal Mayor direct the
MACCO and MGSO involved in undertaking the non-exchange transactions through
donations, grants, transfers without cost of the subject PPEs or other similar manner, to
fast track the transfer of the said properties and to facilitate the timely recording,
complete and proper documentation thereof.

Management’s Comment
The MGSO mentioned in their written reply that they recognized that the PPE totaling
P28,408,958.31 (net depreciation) are indeed beneficially held and controlled by recipient
barangays and public schools. To address this issue, they will:
 Conduct a thorough review of PPE records to identify and segregate assets held by
barangays and public schools.
 Update the records to reflect the correct ownership and control of these assets.
 Implement procedures to transfer assets through donations, transfer without cost to
the different barangays and public schools and to ensure that future acquisitions and
disposal of PPE are properly recorded and reported.

In addition, MAccO also replied that MGSO really did their best to process the transfer
of all PPEs as of December 31, 2023, to the recipient Barangays and Public Schools in 2024,
after which the Accounting Office made the necessary journal entries for derecognition -in the
books. All PPE acquisitions fully controlled by the recipient Barangays and Public Schools
starting 2024 onwards will be processed and transferred in the following year.

b. Recognition in the books of fully depreciated PPEs worth ₱1,892,136.58 (net of
depreciation) were noted.
Fully depreciated PPE with acquired cost amounting to ₱25,545,344.70 as of
December 31, 2020 (details in Appendix B) are still recorded in the books, summarized as
follows:

Management’s Comment

In addition, MGSO will conduct assessment of fully depreciated properties and if the

c. Inappropriate classification of PPE accounts with total acquisition cost of
₱2,240,620.84 (₱1,888,505.59 net of depreciation) were noted.
IPSAS provides descriptions for the Local Government Chart of Accounts which serve
as guide in properly classifying the assets particularly the PPE items. Based from the IPSAS
descriptions, it was learned that 48 various acquisitions from previous years with costs
amounting to ₱2,240,620.84 or ₱1,888,505.59 net of depreciation, (details presented in
Appendix C) should be reclassified to a more befitting PPE classification, as follows:

Upon inquiry with MACCO, it was confirmed that ongoing reclassification of the PPE
accounts have been made by the office, and the entries have been aligned with their appropriate
accounts. MACCO affirmed that the classification of PPE items falls within their office's
responsibilities and is now aligned with budgetary accounts.
We commend MACCO for its actions, but some PPE items still require reclassification.
It should be noted that the office should have carefully considered the nature of each
transaction when accounting for PPE acquisitions. Additionally, related PPE expenses (e.g.,
depreciation, impairment, etc.) should be consistent with the classification of the PPE used.
The effect of the misclassification of PPEs are as follows:

Aside from the guidelines under COA Circular No. 2015-009 dated December 1, 2015–
Prescribing the Revised Chart of Accounts for Local Government Units, the incorrect
classification of the PPE accounts is also contrary to Section 111 (2) of P.D. 1445 which states
that “the highest standards of honesty, objectivity and consistency shall be observed in the
keeping of accounts to safeguard against inaccurate or misleading information.”
Relevant schedules, detailed breakdowns and other pertinent documents to support the
balances of the Province’s PPE must always be properly and completely maintained as these
are used tools in verifying the accuracy, validity and correctness of its presentations in the
financial statements.
In the immediately preceding year, this observation has already been noted but the same
condition of other PPE balances of prior years exist which may require retrospective
restatement pursuant to relevant provision of the IPSAS. Under IPSAS 3, it defined
retrospective restatement as correcting the recognition, measurement, and disclosure of
amounts of elements of financial statements as if a prior period error had never occurred.
We reiterated to recommend and management agreed that the MACCO make
necessary adjustments in the books to correct all inappropriate classifications of PPE
and henceforth apply the principles prescribed in IPSAS 17 and under COA Circular
No. 2015-009 dated December 1, 2015 for proper accounting of the Municipal’s PPE and
for a fair presentation of the asset accounts in the financial statements. The following
adjusting entry is suggested:

Management’s Comment
The MGSO will reclassify PPE accounts to ensure that these are accurately reflected
in the municipal financial statements. They will enhance their procedure to prevent similar
errors in the future.

The MAccO concurred with the observation and noted that adjusting entries had been
made starting January 2025 for items they had already identified—excluding the PPEs
highlighted in the observation—in order to correct the inappropriate classification of property,
plant, and equipment (PPE).

We recommended and management agreed that the Local Chief Executive:
a. direct the Office of the Municipal Agriculture that upon approval of the extension,
promptly update the project implementation plan and initiate activities to meet the
project objectives within the adjusted timeline. Ensure that all project targets and
deliverables are met within the extended period, and ensure timely and complete
liquidation of funds in accordance with government accounting rules and MOA;
b. require the MAccO to monitor the fund status regularly and ensure that unutilized
project funds are reported and disclosed properly in the financial statements, and
require liquidation reports or updates from the OMAG within agreed intervals to
track usage and compliance; and
c. direct the MAccO to conduct periodic evaluation and assessment of the balances
under the Inter-Agency Receivables and Inter-Agency Payables accounts to monitor
its financial status, take up necessary adjusting entries, and to promptly address any
issues on dormant/idle accounts, and confirm balances with the Source Agencies to
ensure the correctness of account balances presented in the financial statements. For
balances deemed no longer collectible or necessary, prepare proper documentation to
support any request for write-off in accordance with applicable government
accounting and auditing rules.

Management’s Comment
Office of the Municipal Agriculturist mentioned in their written reply that they
encountered issues and concerns during the project implementation from the date of fund
release from the DA-RFO 12 to LGU-Surallah which was on September 21, 2023.
They enumerated the following situations:
1. Right of way issues on the construction site of the biosecured facility which took several
months to transfer the property right to LGU-Surallah as donee of the land.
 a. The area of the construction site was donated to the Republic of the Philippines. The
permits and clearances can only be processed if the ownership of the construction
site is under the LGU Surallah, thus the donee was changed from the Republic of
the Philippines to LGU Surallah;
 b. There were discrepancies in the title of the donated land. The land title did not
correspond to the actual location of the construction site. The LGU Surallah
requested to rectify the land title and the issue is already resolved.
2. The water treatment facilities of the project were revised as per PAMB-DENR requirement
for the issuance of PAMB clearance. It took several months to perfect the design and to
defend it to the Protection and Management Board. The DENR will penalize LGU Surallah
if the construction will be started without PAMB clearance. The project is within Protected
Area.
3. The construction site of the biosecured facility is less than 1.0-kilometer radius from the
community and within the protected area (PA), which violates the general zoning guidelines
and ordinance. The LGU Surallah is in the process of the implementation of variance to
reclassify the area for agricultural production area;
4. There were revisions of the structural plans of the facility in order to suit the topographic
condition of the construction site and were completed.
With their innermost desire to implement the project, they diligently facilitated and
complied all necessary documents to pave the way for the construction project and to complete
this ahead of time.
On the other hand, the MAccO responded that concerned departments and program
holders were reminded to fast track the utilization of said amounts (due to NGAs account under
Trust Fund) but due to insufficient funds to finance the intended project/activity, it remained
unused. The management through the Local Finance Committee will call the attention of
concerned individuals and would consider the reversion of the same to the General Fund to be
utilized for other important projects.

In addition, the MAccO mentioned that PTO Undistributed Collections amounting to
₱1,618.40 was already deducted from their remittance to PTO this year. Due from COMELEC
₱7,365.10; PNP-₱574.28; GSIS Burial benefit-₱6,000.00 and GSIS double payment of loan
existed in the books before the Electronic New Government Accounting System was
implemented, and the MAccO could not find any source document that would prove such
claim. They will try to confirm the balances with the source agencies as per audit
recommendation and if a negative feedback is received, they will proceed with the request for
write off.

We recommended and management agreed that the Local Chief Executive:
a. direct the MAccO to conduct detailed review of all insurance premiums paid in
advance to determine the unexpired (prepaid) portion as of December 31, 2024, and
require them to prepare the necessary adjusting journal entries to reflect the
unexpired amount from expenses to the appropriate Prepaid Expense account; and

b. instruct the MAccO to prepare and regularly update a detailed insurance schedule
that consolidates all pertinent details of insurance-related transactions. This should
include list of insured items, coverage periods for each policy, OR numbers, payment
dates, and premium amounts, applicable periods corresponding to each financial year,
JEV number, and other relevant details. This schedule will serve as a monitoring tool
to support accurate recognition of prepaid expenses and adjusting entries at year-end,
and direct them to collaborate with units responsible for vehicle registration, buildings
insurance, and related items to ensure timely receipt of documents and payment
notices.

Management’s Comment
In their written reply, the MAccO are amenable that it has been the practice of their
office to record prepaid insurance as outright expenses and adjusting journal entries to
recognize the asset portion were not prepared at the end of the year. Considering that the March
2025 Financial Statement was already finalized, the necessary adjusting journal entries for the
remaining asset portion from May 2025 up to the end of the period covered, shall be prepared.
All transactions for payment of insurance at the beginning of Calendar Year 2025 onwards
will be recorded as prepaid insurance and adjusting entries will be prepared for the expense
portion at the end of the year.

In closing, they sincerely appreciate the audit observation, and they will put into
practice the recommendations at the earliest possible time to enable to present fairly the
agency's financial condition.

We recommended and management agreed that Management revisit the provisions
of R.A. 656 and COA Circular No. 2018--002 dated May 31, 2018, and regularly prepare
and promptly submit the Property Inventory Form (PIF) to the COA Auditor and GSIS
to ensure that all its insurable assets and properties are fully covered and insured with
General Insurance Fund of GSIS to fully protect them in the event of damage or loss due
to acts of nature, accidents, and/or man-made calamities.

Management’s Comment
In their written reply, the MGSO agreed with the observation and admitted that their
office has not prepared a property Inventory Form (PIF) which is essential for accurately
documenting and valuing assets for insurance purposes. They will go through with their
office’s records and start identifying those assets that were not included in the insured assets
of this municipality. They will prioritize the preparation of the PIF and ensure all insurable
assets are properly documented and covered by insurance to mitigate financial risk.

The office had taken action about those assets under the Buildings account amounting
to ₱29,026,647.31 and have already prepared documents needed for the insurance of this assets
such as Property Insurance Form, Location Sketch Map, Property Inventory Form (Content of
Building) and ready for submission for quotation at the office of the Government Service
Insurance System (GSIS) to prepare for the submission to the Local Finance Committee (LFC)
of this municipality for budgetary action.

Other Structure amounting to ₱21,959,100.83 was already identified and they are
assessing the assets that need to be insured or not, depending on the structure that was
implemented and they seed the assistance of COA Auditor in identifying these assets.

School Buildings amounting to ₱22,323,967.40 were already identified and donation
of assets previously purchased was donated through Resolution No. 67, Series of 2024 issued
by the Sangguniang Bayan dated February 5, 2024. The property, plant and equipment of the
different schools shall be transferred without cost thru Deed of Donation by virtue of this
Sangguniang Bayan Resolution and the office will continue to process documents needed for
the donation and will eventually be dropped from the books of accounts and the inventory
records.

Overall, the Management is actively working to rectify this situation, and this
municipality is committed to addressing this observation and ensuring the long-term protection
of its assets and has initiated a comprehensive review of these assets inventory and they
anticipate having a finalized insurance plan in the following years.

We recommended and management agreed that the MDRRM Council through
the DRRM Officer, in coordination with the Municipal Accounting Department, to fast
track the implementation of the remaining unutilized balances of DRRM Fund coming
from various sources, as a result of fully completed PPAs and consider its re-
appropriation to other priority DRRM PPAs which remained unfunded. Also, evaluate
the status of those unimplemented PPAs if the same is still to be pursued, otherwise
consider its realignment or reversion to other necessary DRRM PPAs.

Management’s Comment
In their written reply, the MDRRMO is in close monitoring of the utilization of the
LDRRM Fund particularly STF of Surallah. The Municipal Accounting Office, Municipal
Budget Office, and MDRRMO established the system to ensure timely and efficient use of
fund. With this system, they ensure the ``first in-first out' of the unused funds and also adhere
to the audit recommendation to consider reappropriation to other priority DRRM PPAs.

We commended the management of the Local School Board (LSB) and the
Municipal Government of Surallah (MGS) for their continued efforts and
commitment to complying with the monitoring, transparency, and accountability
requirements in the allocation and utilization of the Special Education Fund (SEF),
as mandated under Item 6.1 of DepEd-DBM-DILG Joint Circular No. 1, s. 2017.
Their substantial compliance for CY 2024 reflects a proactive approach toward good
governance in education financing.
However, we recommended and management agreed that the Local School
Board:
a) submit the Quarterly and Annual Reports on SEF utilization using the SEF
Budget Accountability Form No. 1 (Annex B) to the Department of Education
Central Office (DepEd CO) through appropriate channels. Copies of the same
should be furnished to the concerned Local Sangguniang Bayan (SB), and the
Regional Offices of the Department of Budget and Management (DBM) and the
Department of the Interior and Local Government (DILG), within the prescribed
deadline pursuant to Item 6.1 of DepEd-DBM-DILG Joint Circular No. 1, Series
of 2017, dated January 19, 2017;
b) integrate the submission and reporting requirements into the Individual
Performance Commitment and Review (IPCR) form of the LSB Secretariat, and
include the same in the Division Performance Commitment of the immediate
supervisor to institutionalize accountability and ensure compliance with set
timelines; and
c) continue the practice of posting the SEF Utilization Reports on the FDP portal
website of the DILG and in at least three (3) conspicuous public places in
accordance with the Full Disclosure Policy, thereby upholding the principles of
transparency and public accessibility.
d) maintain the institutionalization of best practices, periodic review of fund
utilization, and capacity-building initiatives for all stakeholders involved in SEF
management to sustain and further strengthen this positive performance.

Management’s Comment
The Municipal Local School Board take full accountability for every lapse within their
agency. However, they also believe that they deserved to be informed of such a joint circular,
as they had not been practicing the policy for the past two years. They were only made aware
of it during the interview. Nonetheless, since ignorance of the law excuses no one from
compliance, they commit to learning from this experience and doing better moving forward.

classification of all capital expenditures indicated in the approved plans and budget
by harmonizing the same with the revised chart of accounts for LGUs in order to
achieve accurate and reliable reporting in the books compliant with the existing
standards.

We recommended and Management agreed that the Municipal Mayor:
a) direct the Municipal General Service Office and Municipal Accounting Office to
coordinate with each other, ensuring that both offices are aligned in inventory

to Financial Statement the additional information which are not presented on the face of

We reiterated the following recommendations to enhance utilization of the 20%
Local Development Fund and fast track the implementation of PPAs under this fund and
Management agreed that:
a) the MPDO, in coordination with PEO, to develop specific strategies to effectively
implement PPAs, both for current and continuing appropriations. Check
unimplemented PPAs under 20% LDF, determine the causes of non-implementation
and formulate relevant actions to address such; and
b) the Local Development Council to review the unimplemented/unobligated
appropriations of the LDF, determine the status of implementation of the projects by
coordinating with the implementing departments and consider the recommendation
of reversion of the savings from continuing appropriations to be made available for
other priority development projects which shall be derived from the local
development plan.

With the aforementioned deficiencies and in view of its partially implemented
status as reflected in the prior year Annual Audit Report, we reiterated our
recommendations and Management agreed that:
a) the MDRRM Council, headed by the Municipal Mayor, to ensure specific project
identification upon formulation of its annual LDRRMF investment plan (proper
fund utilization and detailed identification of PPAs, time table, labor force,
workload, weather and other related factors) in close coordination with the Local
DRRM Councils (LDRRMCs) for a more timely, effective and integrated
implementation of pre-disaster projects;
b) the implementing departments headed by the Municipal Mayor through the
DRRM Officer, follow the schedule of implementation of pre-disaster PPAs as
embodied in the approved DRRM Investment Plans. In coordination with the
Municipal Engineering Department, evaluate the causes of delays in the
implementation of pre-disaster projects in order to provide immediate resolution
therein and lessen or eliminate the vulnerabilities of the community in a timely
manner; and
c) the MDRRM Council through the DRRM Officer, in coordination with the
Municipal Accounting Department, properly identify the remaining balances of
DRRM Fund coming from various sources, as a result of fully completed PPAs
and consider its re-appropriation to other priority DRRM PPAs which remained
unfunded. Also, evaluate the status of those unimplemented PPAs if the same is
still to be pursued, otherwise consider its realignment or reversion to other
necessary DRRM PPAs.

1. The Municipal Accountant to perform reconciliation of various account
balances in the financial statements particularly the reciprocal accounts

intra-agency receivable and payable accounts to its most appropriate
account title as defined in the Revised Chart of Accounts for LGUs for a
more reliable reporting and monitoring of balances.

In view of the foregoing observation, we reiterated the recommendation
which the Accounting and Treasury Department agreed to monitor and
reconcile regularly the cash balances in their respective records. Any
discrepancies noted and properly justified shall be adjusted accordingly in
either records to reflect the correct and accurate cash balances.

1. The Municipal Treasurer, in coordination with the Accounting
Department, to observe timely the full remittance of collections accruing
to the national government agencies in accordance with existing laws,
rules, and regulations.
2. The implementing offices or departments to either draw up the
appropriate project proposals to utilize fully idle or dormant trust funds,
or to refund excess funds to the National Treasury, or request for
transfer to the General Fund pursuant to law governing said grant
and/or the Memorandum of Agreement/contract with the respective
source agencies.

transfers and collections upon issuance of receipt and the accounting
department to observe proper recording and identification of funds in
the subsidiary ledger by requiring the necessary documents such as
agreements or other related guidelines from the responsible and
concerned department/s or office/s.

financial plan for each collection generated and held in trust for specific

1. The MDRRM Office, through the MDRRM Council, to observe the

Investment Plans are integrated in all respects in the Annual Investment
Program by taking into consideration the appropriate source documents
during consolidation of the AIP to avoid significant differences of
information provided between said plans.

10.21 Therefore, we recommended and the Municipal Accounting Office and the
Municipal Budget Office both agreed to comply with the proper accounting
and reporting guidelines of disaster risk reduction and management fund as
prescribed in the existing laws, rules and regulations such as the
appropriateness of functional classification, reporting of assets procured out
of STF, recording of consumption or issuance of inventories and proper
treatment of unexpended QRF at year end.

that, in coordination with the MPDO and MEBO, to ensure that the program
of works (POWs), project designs, detailed and feasible estimates of funding
needed, coordination with other agencies and other necessary documents
requiring approvals has been prepared and approved without objections

reversion of the savings from continuing appropriations to be made

observations.recommendation:

management agreed to undertake full implementation:
1. The Municipal Accountant to review the unadjusted stale checks as reported
in the Bank Reconciliation Statements and coordinate with the Municipal
Treasurer to apply the required procedures in cancellation in order to take
up proper recording in the books of accounts.

charges per BSP regulations for dormant accounts and below maintaining-
balance bank accounts. Close depository accounts in which the purpose had
already been satisfied to avoid either dormancy charges below or below
maintaining balance bank charges.

Municipal Accountant to stop granting additional cash advances to the
Disbursing Officer until the previous ones have been fully liquidated and/or
settled. Strictly enforce controls in the timely submission, settlement, and
liquidation of cash advances by every Disbursing Officer.

Municipal Treasurer, to settle/liquidate his cash advances immediately upon
serving its purpose and to timely submit complete liquidation documents to
the accounting officer, who in turn shall acknowledge and record
immediately the liquidation documents before closing the books of accounts
at year-end.
We also recommended the following courses of action to address the other
deficiencies noted which they also agreed to implement:
1. The Accounting and Treasury Department to monitor and reconcile
regularly the cash balances in their respective records. Any discrepancies
noted and properly justified shall be adjusted accordingly in either records
to reflect the correct and accurate cash balances.

reliable and regularly-updated cashbooks reflecting real-time balances of
cash in vault and in bank accounts. In addition, the designated Disbursing
Officer/s to timely and regularly update the cashbook, observe complete
reporting of all cash disbursements or payments made during the period and
certify and foot the balances at the end of the month or when required to do
so by proper competent authority.
3. Monitor cash balances on a periodic basis to ensure that restricted funds of
each special account are backed up by cash. Prepare a periodic cash flow
forecast (like, quarterly) to address immediately any noted deficiencies in
cash and in order to allocate sufficient subsidies related thereto.

accounting records.
2. The Municipal Accountant and the Municipal General Services Officer, in
coordination with the concerned departments, analyze the discrepancies
noted in the balances of PPE accounts per physical count and subsidiary
ledger to effect the necessary adjustments in their respective records.
3. The Accounting Department and General Service Department to observe
proper accounting and treatment of all procured PPEs per Revised Chart of
Accounts for Local Government Units as prescribed under COA Circular
2015-009 dated December 01, 2015.
In addition thereto, we also recommended the following courses of action to aid in
the proper recognition of depreciation expense, present accurately the appropriate
carrying amount as of a given period in the financial statement, and to establish PPE
balances that are verifiable as to the existence, condition, and accountability, which they
also agreed to implement:
1. The Municipal Accountant to analyze the discrepancies noted in the balances
of PPE accounts per general ledger and the lapsing schedule of the PPE.
Reconcile the identified discrepancies in both records and record necessary
adjustments to report the correct balances.
2. The Municipal Accountant to review relevant provisions of IPSAS 17 on the
basic features and policies of depreciation on property, plant, and equipment
and observe proper application thereof in every PPE recorded in the books.
Record necessary adjustments in order to reflect the correct balances of
those PPE which are determined to be over or under depreciated.
3. The Municipal Mayor to direct the Inventory Committee together with the
responsible department/s to follow the guidelines and procedures on
inventory-taking, recognition of those found at station, and disposition for
non-existing/missing PPE items for one-time cleansing of PPE accounts of the
municipality as provided under COA Circular 2020-006 dated January 31,
2020.
4. The General Service Office and Inventory Committee to review and follow
the Accounting and Reporting Guidelines on the Local Roads Asset
Management System as provided under COA Circular 2015-008, dated
November 23, 2015.

In view of the foregoing observations, the Local Chief Executive agreed to
undertake the following reiterated courses of action in order to address the observed
delays and non-implementation of Local Development Fund PPAs as well as to hasten
the utilization of the LDF continuing balances:
1. Direct the implementing departments to prioritize the implementation of
development projects and ensure that allocated funds are timely and
optimally utilized for projects identified in accordance with the guidelines
prescribed under the DILG-DBM JMC No. 2017-01.
2. Require the Local Development Council to review the
unobligated/unprogrammed appropriations of the LDF, determine the status
of implementation of the projects by coordinating with the implementing
departments and consider the recommendation of reversion of the savings
from continuing appropriations to be made available for other priority
development projects which shall be derived from the local development
plan.
In addition, it was also agreed that the following recommendation be undertaken
to address the noted deficiency on the allocation of 20% Development Fund:
1. Require the Local Development Council to refrain from including program,
projects and activities that do not partake the nature of investments and
capital expenditures in the annual or supplemental investment programs
pursuant to DILG-DBM Joint Memorandum Circular No. 2017-1 dated
February 22, 2017. Henceforth, analyze every project to be funded by 20%
DF to identify and provide appropriate account classification of the expenses
allocated on each project.

During the exit conference, the management informed that the audit findings were
well taken and committed to undertake the following recommendations:
1. The Municipal Accountant’s Office to maintain separate subsidiary ledgers
for transfers of municipality’s unutilized DRRMF to the special trust fund as
mandated by COA Circular No. 2012-002 dated September 12, 2012.
2. The MDRRM Council to ensure that all PPAs charged against the Special
Trust Fund are included in the approved LDRRM investment plan. The
Accounting Department to maintain separate subsidiary ledger of other
receipts not pertaining to transfers of unexpended balances of DRRM funds
such as prizes or incentives or cash donations in order to ensure proper
planning, monitoring and utilization of available funds.
3. The Municipal Treasurer to coordinate and submit the supporting
documents to the Municipal Accounting Office to reconcile the unadjusted
cancelled check and to take up necessary adjustments to properly report the
cash balance.
In addition thereto, we also reiterated the following recommendations which the
management also agreed to implement:
1. The MDRRM Council through the DRRM Officer to periodically analyze the
remaining balances of DRRM Fund and its related PPAs. Such remaining
balances and identified DRRM idle funds should be immediately planned
based on the priority PPAs as identified by the DRRM Council.

With the aforementioned deficiencies and in view of its partially implemented
status as reflected in the prior year Annual Audit Report, the following recommendations
are reiterated which the Local Chie Executive agreed to:
1. Require the Municipal Treasurer, in coordination with the accounting
department, to observe timely and full remittance of all collections accruing
to the national government in accordance with the existing laws, rules and
regulations.
2. Require the implementing offices or departments to either draw up the
appropriate project proposals to utilize fully idle or dormant trust funds or
to refund excess project funds to the national treasury or request for transfer
to the General Fund pursuant to the law governing said grant and/or the
Memorandum of Agreement/contract with the respective source agencies.
3. Require the Accounting Department, in coordination with the implementing
departments, to monitor and analyze the records in the ledger and properly
classify and specifically identify those inter-agency transferred funds and
collections. Henceforth, the treasury department to provide specific
identification of fund transfers and collections upon issuance of receipt and
the accounting department to observe proper recording and identification of
funds in the ledger by requiring the necessary documents such as agreements
or other related guidelines from the responsible and concerned department/s
or offices.
4. Require the implementing departments to provide an approved work and
financial plan for each collection generated and held in trust for specific
purposes. Execute without delay the intended programs, projects and
activities of the fund transfers and other specific-purpose collections so that
the benefits expected therefrom may already be enjoyed by the intended
beneficiaries.
In addition thereto, the following new recommendations were given to which
management agreed to implement:
5. The Accounting Department, in coordination with the implementing
departments to perform reconciliation and record the necessary adjusting
entries of those dormant/non-moving fund transfers. Once reconciled but
there are still remaining unadjusted balances, gather the required documents
necessary to file the request for authority to write-off dormant fund transfers
such as certification by the chief accountants and approved by the heads of
the source and implementing agencies that the fund was utilized for the
purpose, and certification from the recipient that the project was partially or
fully implemented, supported by pictures of the implemented projects.

In their written reply, the management committed to review the deficiencies noted
and to undertake the following recommendation to comply with the tax laws and
regulations:
1. The Municipal Mayor to direct the Municipal Accountant and the
Municipal Treasurer to timely and accurately remit all the taxes withheld by
the municipal government to avoid under/over remittance and incur
penalties and surcharges pursuant to existing tax laws and regulations. With
regard to those taxes previously withheld but were not remitted due to lack
of information, exert diligent efforts to have it available for immediate
remittance thereof. Henceforth, institute relevant controls in order to gather
the necessary and required information pertaining to withholding taxes
previously made.

Accordingly, we recommended the following courses of actions which the
concerned departments agreed to implement:
1. The Accounting Department and the MDRRM Office to use the Welfare
Goods Expenses account to record the cost of goods issued/distributed to
people affected by calamities/disasters/ground conflicts for appropriate
identification in their respective records. Henceforth, direct the Accounting
Department, in coordination with the implementing departments, to provide
the proper account identification of various expenditures to be funded under
the approved annual and supplemental budget/s based on the Revised Chart
of Accounts for LGUs;
2. The concerned offices (MDRRM Office, General Services Office, Treasurer’s
Office, and Accounting Office) to institute controls in the receipt, recording,
inventory, storage and distribution of all donations received and to submit
the required reports together with the supporting documents mandated by
COA Circular No. 2020-009 dated April 21, 2020;
3. The Treasury and Accounting Department to closely coordinate with each
other with respect to proper identification of specific-purpose receipts such
as cash donations intended to support disaster-related PPAs. The Treasury
Department, in coordination with the implementing departments, to indicate
in the official receipt or other related document the intended purpose of the
donations/assistance actually receipted in order to have proper reporting,
monitoring and timely implementation thereof.
4. The MDRRM Council through the DRRM Officer to provide a written
justification on the grant of financial assistance to barangay officials,
employees and staff and the basis of distinction made or non-uniformity of
payment between the PBs and other barangay officials and employees.
Submit supporting documents such as order authorizing the employees to
physically report for work approved by Local Chief Executive, copy of duties
and responsibilities of personnel during the period of grant and the
corresponding report thereon, and daily time records.

1. The Municipal Accountant to review the unadjusted stale checks as reported in the Bank Reconciliation Statements and coordinate with the Municipal Treasurer to apply the required procedures in cancellation in order to take up proper recording in the books of accounts.

2. The Municipal Accountant to monitor regularly the cash balances to prevent charges per BSP regulations for dormant accounts and below maintaining-balance bank accounts. Close depository accounts in which the purpose had already been satisfied to avoid either dormancy charges below or below maintaining balance bank charges.

procedures provided under COA Circular 2016-005 dated December 19, 2016.

dormant fund transfers to the proper officials as provided in the circular.

observations.recommendation:
Recommendations
We recommended the following:
Require all concerned officials and employees to liquidate immediately their outstanding cash advances and observe strictly the rules and regulations regarding the granting and liquidation of cash advance.
A close monitoring of the outstanding cash advances shall be done by the Municipal Accountant. Demand letters shall be issued for the immediate liquidation or settlement of the same. Enforce sanctions under COA Circular No. 97-002, when deem appropriate.
For unliquidated cash advances which remained non-moving for ten (10) years or more and where settlement/collectability could no longer be ascertained, request for the write-off of these unliquidated cash advances. Follow the guidelines and procedures stated in COA Circular No. 2016-005 dated December 19, 2016.

Recommendations
We recommended the following:
Retrieve all records and reports that would establish validity and existence of the receivables and determine the liable officers or persons.
Initiate available legal action, if the amount is material, to collect and recover the aging and dormant accounts receivables from the entities and persons liable.
Request for the write-off of receivables which remained non-moving for ten (10) years or more and where settlement/collectibility could no longer be ascertained. Follow the guidelines and procedures stated in COA Circular No. 2016-005 dated December 19, 2016.

Recommendations
We recommended the following:
The Municipal Accountant should exert effort in fast tracking all the adjusting entries transferring finished projects of road networks to the Registry of Public Infrastructures. This is to identify the complete list of those recorded in the registry for reconciliation with the list of actual physical inventory and eventual recording as PPE.
Require the management to create an Inventory Committee to conduct the physical count of all road networks under their jurisdiction and prepare the Report on the Physical Count of the Road Network System as provided in the Annex C of the COA Circular 2015-008 dated November 25, 2015. From the Inventory Report, segregate each component of road networks and identify its respective costs and estimated useful life for the computation of depreciation expense.
Advise the Municipal Accountant, Engineer, General Service Officer and other concerned officials to adhere to the COA Circular 2015-008 for their duties and responsibilities in the transition of the Local Road Network System for the adaptation of the PPSAS and follow the accounting and procedures provided therein.

Recommendations
We recommended the following:
Management should require the Accountant and General Service Officer to strictly adhere to the provisions set forth in the Chapter 7, Sections 112 to 124 of the New Government Accounting System for Local Government Units for the Accounting of Supplies or Property;
Also, management should require the GSO to maintain reliable and complete records for inventory and reports as prescribed by the said Manual, particularly the submission of RPCI through regular conduct of physical count.

Recommendations
We recommended the following:
1. Management should require GSO and Accounting Department to reconcile balances between the RPCPPE and accounting records. Any discrepancies between the records of two offices should be investigated and adjusted accordingly.
2. Likewise, GSO should conduct complete physical inventory of PPE in accordance with Section 124 of NGAS Volume I. This will enable the municipality to provide a readily verifiable valuation of properties as reported in the financial statements.

Recommendations
We recommended the following:
Management should instruct the GSO to dispose the unserviceable properties pursuant to Section 79 of P.D. 1445 and instruct the Municipal Accountant to account the same as guided by PPSAS 17, Property, Plant and Equipment.
Also, GSO should report immediately lost properties and request for subsequent relief of accountability to be able to establish correct balances in the financial statements.

Recommendation
We recommended that the Municipal Accountant should confirm from the creditors their claims against the LGU, and require them to submit documents supporting their claims. In the event that the suppliers fail to support their claims, the reversion should be undertaken in accordance with Section 98 of PD 1445.

Recommendations
We recommended the following:
Maximize the utilization of the 20% Development Fund allocated for the implementation of various projects, programs and activities to achieve the desirable socio-economic development and environmental outcomes.
Through a Sanggunian Bayan Resolution, exclude the projects amounting to ₱17,600,000.00 from the total continuing appropriation since this is not supported with cash.

Recommendation
We recommended that verification shall be done by the Accountant to determine any existing obligation against the unexpended balance of the completed projects. In case that there are no existing obligations to be paid therefrom, reversion to the Unappropriated Surplus of general fund shall be made by the Budget Officer to be appropriated to other priority projects necessary in public service.

Instruct the Municipal Accountant to take-up in the books of accounts, the stale checks which were already more than two years as surplus adjustment of the fund against which these were previously charged in accordance with Section 97.1 of the PD 1445 otherwise known as the Government Auditing Code of the Philippines.

From then on, require the Municipal Treasurer to send written notices to all the payees of the existence of the check before these will become stale.  The Treasurer shall mark all unclaimed stale check in her possession/custody as “CANCELLED”. The original disbursement vouchers and supporting documents shall be returned/transmitted to the Municipal Accountant who shall prepare a Journal Entry Voucher to record the transaction as Accounts Payable.

Instruct the collecting officers to turn over their collection to their designated liquidating officer daily or the next following day. The liquidating officer shall perform procedures for the receipt and verification of collections turned over to him and shall turn over intact the cash collection to the Treasurer/Cashier. The Treasurer/Cashier shall deposit all the collections with the authorized depository bank pursuant to Section 32 of the Manual on the New Government Accounting System for Local Government Units.

Require the Head of the Human Resource Management Office, who prepared the payroll, to strictly observe Section 48 of the General Provisions of General Appropriation Act of 2015 in preparing the monthly payroll to ensure that net take home pay will not reach below the required minimum amount.

Follow the guidelines/age requirement set by the Department of Social Welfare and Development (DSWD) in the implementation of the Social Pension Program in the municipality to ensure that the Social Pension Program will be availed by those eligible to be social pensioners. Submit a written justification explaining why Senior Citizens below 65 years old were given social pensions under the Social Pension Program and that no notice of disallowance be issued.

Consider other strategies in releasing the stipend to the beneficiaries provided they are in accordance with the guidelines and budget rules and regulations such as those enumerated in Administrative Order No.04, Series of 2012 issued by the Department of Social Welfare and Development (DSWD), to wit:
Conduct pay-out thru a barangay assembly and at a venue (plaza) accessible to the beneficiaries.
Coordinate with the senior citizens organization.
Personal delivery of stipend to the house of the beneficiary by the social worker.

Utilize first the proceeds from sale of bid documents and other related fees collected in the payment of honoraria of the members of the BAC, TWG, and BAC Secretariat before sourcing out from the savings of the Municipality since honoraria are not considered mandatory expenditure items which will be given priority in the use of savings.

Transfer any excess in the amount collected from sources enumerated in Section 3.1.1 of DBM Budget Circular No. 2007-3 to form part of the local government funds, starting with the unutilized collections as of December 31, 2015.

Comply strictly with the documentary requirements provided in COA Circular No.2012-001 on the grant of honoraria to officers/employees involved in government procurement. Notice of Suspension will be issued.

Instruct the In-charge of Accountable Forms/Municipal Treasurer to verify the existence of the inventory. If the inventory of accountable forms were already issued, require her to prepare an Invoice of Receipt of Accountable Forms and submit it to the Accounting Division as basis for the preparation of JEV to derecognize the inventory from the books of accounts.

2. Advise the Accounting Division to recognize the purchase of Accountable Forms as Inventory and recognize as expense only upon its issuance based on the reports submitted by the In-charge of Accountable Forms.

3. Require the In-charge of Accountable Forms to prepare Monthly Consolidated Reports of Accountability for Accountable Forms, together with the necessary documents, such as Invoice Receipts of Accountable Forms issued to the Accountable Officers and the Summary of Supplies and Materials Issued and submit the same to the Accounting and Auditing Offices.

Impose the provision of Section 68 of the Implementing Rules and Regulations of R.A. 9184  for  delayed deliveries of procurement of goods and services.                                                                                                                                                                                                                                          Likewise, collect the amount of P24,337.93 from Shemarie Construction Supply due to delayed deliveries of construction materials.

Comply strictly with the rules and regulations relative to the use of government vehicles, particularly in the preparation and submission of duly accomplished and approved Monthly Report of Official Travels and Report of Fuel Consumption.
